title: ST 558 Homework 3
text:
You should specify the path: "../MyData.csv". The .. tells R to move up one level out of the current folder homework/ and into the parent folder myfolder/.
Using R projects increases the “portability” of your work by keeping necessary files in a common directory for use with version control software or sharing with collaborators
Git is a version control system that tracks changes to files over time. It allows you to:


Save snapshots of your work (commits)
View the history of changes
Revert to previous versions if something goes wrong
Work on different features independently using branches
Merge changes from multiple contributors

GitHub is a cloud-based platform that hosts Git repositories online. It allows you to:

Store and back up your Git repositories
Collaborate with others on projects
Share code publicly or privately
Review changes through pull requests
Track issues and manage projects


Tibbles:

Print nicer than data frames
Do not simplify to a vector when a single column is referenced

Here’s the same code with chaining:


# shut up tidyverse
suppressMessages(library(tidyverse))

as_tibble(iris) |>
  select(starts_with("Petal"), Species) |>
  filter(Petal.Length < 1.55) |>
  arrange(Species) |>
  head()

# A tibble: 6 × 3
  Petal.Length Petal.Width Species
         <dbl>       <dbl> <fct>  
1          1.4         0.2 setosa 
2          1.4         0.2 setosa 
3          1.3         0.2 setosa 
4          1.5         0.2 setosa 
5          1.4         0.2 setosa 
6          1.4         0.3 setosa 



Long format data has multiple rows for a single record, with one column holding variable names and another holding the values. Wide format data has all variables and repeated measurements for a single observational unit spread across a single row. For most statistical analyses, we generally prefer long format data because it is easier to work with and is compatible with many statistical modeling and visualization functions.

title: ST 558 Homework 3
text:
You should specify the path: "../MyData.csv". The .. tells R to move up one level out of the current folder homework/ and into the parent folder myfolder/.
Using R projects increases the “portability” of your work by keeping necessary files in a common directory for use with version control software or sharing with collaborators
Git is a version control system that tracks changes to files over time. It allows you to:


Save snapshots of your work (commits)
View the history of changes
Revert to previous versions if something goes wrong
Work on different features independently using branches
Merge changes from multiple contributors

GitHub is a cloud-based platform that hosts Git repositories online. It allows you to:

Store and back up your Git repositories
Collaborate with others on projects
Share code publicly or privately
Review changes through pull requests
Track issues and manage projects


Tibbles:

Print nicer than data frames
Do not simplify to a vector when a single column is referenced

Here’s the same code with chaining:


# shut up tidyverse
suppressMessages(library(tidyverse))

as_tibble(iris) |>
  select(starts_with("Petal"), Species) |>
  filter(Petal.Length < 1.55) |>
  arrange(Species) |>
  head()

# A tibble: 6 × 3
  Petal.Length Petal.Width Species
         <dbl>       <dbl> <fct>  
1          1.4         0.2 setosa 
2          1.4         0.2 setosa 
3          1.3         0.2 setosa 
4          1.5         0.2 setosa 
5          1.4         0.2 setosa 
6          1.4         0.3 setosa 



Long format data has multiple rows for a single record, with one column holding variable names and another holding the values. Wide format data has all variables and repeated measurements for a single observational unit spread across a single row. For most statistical analyses, we generally prefer long format data because it is easier to work with and is compatible with many statistical modeling and visualization functions.

title: ST 558 Homework 3
text:
Task 2: Reading Delimited Data

Glass Data
We’ll start by reading in the glass.csv file:

glass_names <- c("id", "ri", "na", "mg", "al", "si", "k", "ca", "ba", "fe", "type")

glass <- read_csv("https://www4.stat.ncsu.edu/online/datasets/glass.data",
                  col_names = glass_names,
                  show_col_types = FALSE)

glass

# A tibble: 214 × 11
      id    ri    na    mg    al    si     k    ca    ba    fe  type
   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>
 1     1  1.52  13.6  4.49  1.1   71.8  0.06  8.75     0  0        1
 2     2  1.52  13.9  3.6   1.36  72.7  0.48  7.83     0  0        1
 3     3  1.52  13.5  3.55  1.54  73.0  0.39  7.78     0  0        1
 4     4  1.52  13.2  3.69  1.29  72.6  0.57  8.22     0  0        1
 5     5  1.52  13.3  3.62  1.24  73.1  0.55  8.07     0  0        1
 6     6  1.52  12.8  3.61  1.62  73.0  0.64  8.07     0  0.26     1
 7     7  1.52  13.3  3.6   1.14  73.1  0.58  8.17     0  0        1
 8     8  1.52  13.2  3.61  1.05  73.2  0.57  8.24     0  0        1
 9     9  1.52  14.0  3.58  1.37  72.1  0.56  8.3      0  0        1
10    10  1.52  13    3.6   1.36  73.0  0.57  8.4      0  0.11     1
# ℹ 204 more rows


Our tibble looks good, but we need to recode our type variable.
I wanted to use a switch statement to recode the levels, but those don’t work inside of mutate for some reason. I found case_match in the dplyr documentation as a vectorized alternative so we’re using it here.

glass |>
  mutate(glass, type = factor(type,
                              levels = 1:7,
                              labels = c("building_windows_float_processed",
                                         "building_windows_non_float_processed",
                                         "vehicle_windows_float_processed",
                                         "vehicle_windows_non_float_processed",
                                         "containers",
                                         "tableware",
                                         "headlamps"))) |>
                filter(fe < 0.2, type %in% c("tableware", "headlamps"))

# A tibble: 38 × 11
      id    ri    na    mg    al    si     k    ca    ba    fe type     
   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <fct>    
 1   177  1.52  14    2.39  1.56  72.4  0     9.57  0        0 tableware
 2   178  1.52  13.8  2.41  1.19  72.8  0     9.77  0        0 tableware
 3   179  1.52  14.5  2.24  1.62  72.4  0     9.26  0        0 tableware
 4   180  1.52  14.1  2.19  1.66  72.7  0     9.32  0        0 tableware
 5   181  1.51  14.4  1.74  1.54  74.6  0     7.59  0        0 tableware
 6   182  1.52  15.0  0.78  1.74  72.5  0     9.95  0        0 tableware
 7   183  1.52  14.2  0     2.09  72.7  0    10.9   0        0 tableware
 8   184  1.52  14.6  0     0.56  73.5  0    11.2   0        0 tableware
 9   185  1.51  17.4  0     0.34  75.4  0     6.65  0        0 tableware
10   186  1.51  13.7  3.2   1.81  72.8  1.76  5.43  1.19     0 headlamps
# ℹ 28 more rows




Yeast Data
Start by reading in the fixed-width data as shown in the video:

yeast_names <- c("seq_name", "meg", "gvh", "alm", "mit", "erl", "pox", "vac", "nuc", "class")

yeast <- read_fwf("https://www4.stat.ncsu.edu/online/datasets/yeast.data",
                  col_positions = fwf_widths(c(12, 6, 6, 6, 6, 6, 6, 6, 6, 3),
                                             col_names = yeast_names),
                  show_col_types = FALSE)

yeast

# A tibble: 1,484 × 10
   seq_name     meg   gvh   alm   mit   erl   pox   vac   nuc class
   <chr>      <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <chr>
 1 ADT1_YEAST  0.58  0.61  0.47  0.13   0.5   0    0.48  0.22 MIT  
 2 ADT2_YEAST  0.43  0.67  0.48  0.27   0.5   0    0.53  0.22 MIT  
 3 ADT3_YEAST  0.64  0.62  0.49  0.15   0.5   0    0.53  0.22 MIT  
 4 AAR2_YEAST  0.58  0.44  0.57  0.13   0.5   0    0.54  0.22 NUC  
 5 AATM_YEAST  0.42  0.44  0.48  0.54   0.5   0    0.48  0.22 MIT  
 6 AATC_YEAST  0.51  0.4   0.56  0.17   0.5   0.5  0.49  0.22 CYT  
 7 ABC1_YEAST  0.5   0.54  0.48  0.65   0.5   0    0.53  0.22 MIT  
 8 BAF1_YEAST  0.48  0.45  0.59  0.2    0.5   0    0.58  0.34 NUC  
 9 ABF2_YEAST  0.55  0.5   0.66  0.36   0.5   0    0.49  0.22 MIT  
10 ABP1_YEAST  0.4   0.39  0.6   0.15   0.5   0    0.58  0.3  CYT  
# ℹ 1,474 more rows


Now we can subset the columns and create the summaries. I’m barely changing the code from the video:

yeast_sub <- yeast |>
              select(-c("seq_name", "nuc")) |>
              group_by(class) |>
              mutate(across(where(is.numeric), list(mean = mean, median = median),
                            .names = "{.col}_{.fn}"))

yeast_sub

# A tibble: 1,484 × 22
# Groups:   class [10]
     meg   gvh   alm   mit   erl   pox   vac class meg_mean meg_median gvh_mean
   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <chr>    <dbl>      <dbl>    <dbl>
 1  0.58  0.61  0.47  0.13   0.5   0    0.48 MIT      0.521       0.51    0.533
 2  0.43  0.67  0.48  0.27   0.5   0    0.53 MIT      0.521       0.51    0.533
 3  0.64  0.62  0.49  0.15   0.5   0    0.53 MIT      0.521       0.51    0.533
 4  0.58  0.44  0.57  0.13   0.5   0    0.54 NUC      0.452       0.45    0.456
 5  0.42  0.44  0.48  0.54   0.5   0    0.48 MIT      0.521       0.51    0.533
 6  0.51  0.4   0.56  0.17   0.5   0.5  0.49 CYT      0.481       0.48    0.470
 7  0.5   0.54  0.48  0.65   0.5   0    0.53 MIT      0.521       0.51    0.533
 8  0.48  0.45  0.59  0.2    0.5   0    0.58 NUC      0.452       0.45    0.456
 9  0.55  0.5   0.66  0.36   0.5   0    0.49 MIT      0.521       0.51    0.533
10  0.4   0.39  0.6   0.15   0.5   0    0.58 CYT      0.481       0.48    0.470
# ℹ 1,474 more rows
# ℹ 11 more variables: gvh_median <dbl>, alm_mean <dbl>, alm_median <dbl>,
#   mit_mean <dbl>, mit_median <dbl>, erl_mean <dbl>, erl_median <dbl>,
#   pox_mean <dbl>, pox_median <dbl>, vac_mean <dbl>, vac_median <dbl>


Great, we have our summary columns for each variable grouped by the class.

title: ST 558 Homework 3
text:
Task 3: Joining Data
We start by importing the wine data from the first sheet of the excel workbook we’re provided:

library(readxl)

white_wine <- read_xlsx("white-wine.xlsx",
                        sheet = 1)

white_wine

# A tibble: 4,898 × 12
   `fixed acidity` `volatile acidity` `citric acid` `residual sugar` chlorides
             <dbl>              <dbl>         <dbl>            <dbl>     <dbl>
 1               7               0.27          0.36             20.7     0.045
 2              63               0.3           0.34              1.6     0.049
 3              81               0.28          0.4               6.9     0.05 
 4              72               0.23          0.32              8.5     0.058
 5              72               0.23          0.32              8.5     0.058
 6              81               0.28          0.4               6.9     0.05 
 7              62               0.32          0.16              7       0.045
 8               7               0.27          0.36             20.7     0.045
 9              63               0.3           0.34              1.6     0.049
10              81               0.22          0.43              1.5     0.044
# ℹ 4,888 more rows
# ℹ 7 more variables: `free sulfur dioxide` <dbl>,
#   `total sulfur dioxide` <dbl>, density <dbl>, pH <dbl>, sulphates <dbl>,
#   alcohol <dbl>, quality <dbl>


We don’t want variable names with spaces in them. Let’s pull better names from the second sheet and replace these weird ones.

wine_names <- read_xlsx("white-wine.xlsx",
                        sheet = 2) |>
              pull()

colnames(white_wine) <- wine_names

white_wine

# A tibble: 4,898 × 12
   fixed_acidity volatile_acidity citric_acid residual_sugar chlorides
           <dbl>            <dbl>       <dbl>          <dbl>     <dbl>
 1             7             0.27        0.36           20.7     0.045
 2            63             0.3         0.34            1.6     0.049
 3            81             0.28        0.4             6.9     0.05 
 4            72             0.23        0.32            8.5     0.058
 5            72             0.23        0.32            8.5     0.058
 6            81             0.28        0.4             6.9     0.05 
 7            62             0.32        0.16            7       0.045
 8             7             0.27        0.36           20.7     0.045
 9            63             0.3         0.34            1.6     0.049
10            81             0.22        0.43            1.5     0.044
# ℹ 4,888 more rows
# ℹ 7 more variables: free_sulfur_dioxide <dbl>, total_sulfur_dioxide <dbl>,
#   density <dbl>, pH <dbl>, sulphates <dbl>, alcohol <dbl>, quality <dbl>


Great! We just need to add the type of wine to each observation:

white_wine$type <- "White"

# preview
white_wine[,10:13]

# A tibble: 4,898 × 4
   sulphates alcohol quality type 
       <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl> <chr>
 1      0.45      88       6 White
 2      0.49      95       6 White
 3      0.44     101       6 White
 4      0.4       99       6 White
 5      0.4       99       6 White
 6      0.44     101       6 White
 7      0.47      96       6 White
 8      0.45      88       6 White
 9      0.49      95       6 White
10      0.45      11       6 White
# ℹ 4,888 more rows


Now that the white wine data is complete, we can turn our attention to the reds:

red_wine <- read_delim("https://www4.stat.ncsu.edu/~online/datasets/red-wine.csv",
                       skip = 1,
                       delim = ";",
                       col_names = wine_names)

Rows: 1599 Columns: 12
── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Delimiter: ";"
dbl (12): fixed_acidity, volatile_acidity, citric_acid, residual_sugar, chlo...

ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.

red_wine$type <- "red"

red_wine

# A tibble: 1,599 × 13
   fixed_acidity volatile_acidity citric_acid residual_sugar chlorides
           <dbl>            <dbl>       <dbl>          <dbl>     <dbl>
 1           7.4             0.7         0               1.9     0.076
 2           7.8             0.88        0               2.6     0.098
 3           7.8             0.76        0.04            2.3     0.092
 4          11.2             0.28        0.56            1.9     0.075
 5           7.4             0.7         0               1.9     0.076
 6           7.4             0.66        0               1.8     0.075
 7           7.9             0.6         0.06            1.6     0.069
 8           7.3             0.65        0               1.2     0.065
 9           7.8             0.58        0.02            2       0.073
10           7.5             0.5         0.36            6.1     0.071
# ℹ 1,589 more rows
# ℹ 8 more variables: free_sulfur_dioxide <dbl>, total_sulfur_dioxide <dbl>,
#   density <dbl>, pH <dbl>, sulphates <dbl>, alcohol <dbl>, quality <dbl>,
#   type <chr>


Perfect! With the correct column types and names, it’s easy to merge our two data sets:

wine <- bind_rows(white_wine, red_wine)

wine

# A tibble: 6,497 × 13
   fixed_acidity volatile_acidity citric_acid residual_sugar chlorides
           <dbl>            <dbl>       <dbl>          <dbl>     <dbl>
 1             7             0.27        0.36           20.7     0.045
 2            63             0.3         0.34            1.6     0.049
 3            81             0.28        0.4             6.9     0.05 
 4            72             0.23        0.32            8.5     0.058
 5            72             0.23        0.32            8.5     0.058
 6            81             0.28        0.4             6.9     0.05 
 7            62             0.32        0.16            7       0.045
 8             7             0.27        0.36           20.7     0.045
 9            63             0.3         0.34            1.6     0.049
10            81             0.22        0.43            1.5     0.044
# ℹ 6,487 more rows
# ℹ 8 more variables: free_sulfur_dioxide <dbl>, total_sulfur_dioxide <dbl>,
#   density <dbl>, pH <dbl>, sulphates <dbl>, alcohol <dbl>, quality <dbl>,
#   type <chr>


Lastly, we’ll make a pipeline using the combined data set:

wine |>
  filter(quality > 6.5, alcohol < 132) |> # only 6.5+ quality, less than 132 alcohol
  arrange(desc(quality)) |>                         # arrange in desc quality order
  select(contains("acid"), alcohol, type, quality) |>      # subset columns
  group_by(quality) |>                                    # group by quality
  mutate(across(alcohol, list(mean = mean, sd = sd),    # add alcohol mean/sd columns
                .names = "{.fn}_alcohol"))

# A tibble: 1,206 × 8
# Groups:   quality [3]
   fixed_acidity volatile_acidity citric_acid alcohol type  quality mean_alcohol
           <dbl>            <dbl>       <dbl>   <dbl> <chr>   <dbl>        <dbl>
 1            91             0.27        0.45     104 White       9        122. 
 2            66             0.36        0.29     124 White       9        122. 
 3            74             0.24        0.36     125 White       9        122. 
 4            69             0.36        0.34     127 White       9        122. 
 5            71             0.26        0.49     129 White       9        122. 
 6            62             0.66        0.48     128 White       8         94.1
 7            62             0.66        0.48     128 White       8         94.1
 8            68             0.26        0.42     105 White       8         94.1
 9            67             0.23        0.31     107 White       8         94.1
10            67             0.23        0.31     107 White       8         94.1
# ℹ 1,196 more rows
# ℹ 1 more variable: sd_alcohol <dbl>

title: ST 558 Homework 3
text:
Task 4: Database Practice
Connecting to the lahman database is quite easy:

library(DBI)

lahman <- dbConnect(RSQLite::SQLite(), "lahman.db")

dbListTables(lahman)

 [1] "AllstarFull"         "Appearances"         "AwardsManagers"     
 [4] "AwardsPlayers"       "AwardsShareManagers" "AwardsSharePlayers" 
 [7] "Batting"             "BattingPost"         "CollegePlaying"     
[10] "Fielding"            "FieldingOF"          "FieldingOFsplit"    
[13] "FieldingPost"        "HallOfFame"          "HomeGames"          
[16] "LahmanData"          "Managers"            "ManagersHalf"       
[19] "Parks"               "People"              "Pitching"           
[22] "PitchingPost"        "Salaries"            "Schools"            
[25] "SeriesPost"          "Teams"               "TeamsFranchises"    
[28] "TeamsHalf"           "battingLabels"       "fieldingLabels"     
[31] "pitchingLabels"     


There are a lot of tables within this database!
We start by querying the Teams table and returning observations from the year 2015:

data_2015 <- tbl(lahman, "Teams") |>
               filter(yearID == 2015) |>
               collect()

data_2015

# A tibble: 30 × 48
   yearID lgID  teamID franchID divID  Rank     G Ghome     W     L DivWin WCWin
    <int> <chr> <chr>  <chr>    <chr> <int> <int> <int> <int> <int> <chr>  <chr>
 1   2015 NL    ARI    ARI      W         3   162    81    79    83 N      N    
 2   2015 NL    ATL    ATL      E         4   162    81    67    95 N      N    
 3   2015 AL    BAL    BAL      E         3   162    78    81    81 N      N    
 4   2015 AL    BOS    BOS      E         5   162    81    78    84 N      N    
 5   2015 AL    CHA    CHW      C         4   162    81    76    86 N      N    
 6   2015 NL    CHN    CHC      C         3   162    81    97    65 N      Y    
 7   2015 NL    CIN    CIN      C         5   162    81    64    98 N      N    
 8   2015 AL    CLE    CLE      C         3   161    80    81    80 N      N    
 9   2015 NL    COL    COL      W         5   162    81    68    94 N      N    
10   2015 AL    DET    DET      C         5   161    81    74    87 N      N    
# ℹ 20 more rows
# ℹ 36 more variables: LgWin <chr>, WSWin <chr>, R <int>, AB <int>, H <int>,
#   X2B <int>, X3B <int>, HR <int>, BB <int>, SO <int>, SB <int>, CS <int>,
#   HBP <int>, SF <int>, RA <int>, ER <int>, ERA <dbl>, CG <int>, SHO <int>,
#   SV <int>, IPouts <int>, HA <int>, HRA <int>, BBA <int>, SOA <int>, E <int>,
#   DP <int>, FP <dbl>, name <chr>, park <chr>, attendance <int>, BPF <int>,
#   PPF <int>, teamIDBR <chr>, teamIDlahman45 <chr>, teamIDretro <chr>


We can do the same thing using SQL commands instead:

data_2015_frmsql <- tbl(lahman, sql(
                        "SELECT *
                           FROM `Teams`
                           WHERE (`yearID` = 2015)"
                        )) |>
                    collect()

data_2015_frmsql

# A tibble: 30 × 48
   yearID lgID  teamID franchID divID  Rank     G Ghome     W     L DivWin WCWin
    <int> <chr> <chr>  <chr>    <chr> <int> <int> <int> <int> <int> <chr>  <chr>
 1   2015 NL    ARI    ARI      W         3   162    81    79    83 N      N    
 2   2015 NL    ATL    ATL      E         4   162    81    67    95 N      N    
 3   2015 AL    BAL    BAL      E         3   162    78    81    81 N      N    
 4   2015 AL    BOS    BOS      E         5   162    81    78    84 N      N    
 5   2015 AL    CHA    CHW      C         4   162    81    76    86 N      N    
 6   2015 NL    CHN    CHC      C         3   162    81    97    65 N      Y    
 7   2015 NL    CIN    CIN      C         5   162    81    64    98 N      N    
 8   2015 AL    CLE    CLE      C         3   161    80    81    80 N      N    
 9   2015 NL    COL    COL      W         5   162    81    68    94 N      N    
10   2015 AL    DET    DET      C         5   161    81    74    87 N      N    
# ℹ 20 more rows
# ℹ 36 more variables: LgWin <chr>, WSWin <chr>, R <int>, AB <int>, H <int>,
#   X2B <int>, X3B <int>, HR <int>, BB <int>, SO <int>, SB <int>, CS <int>,
#   HBP <int>, SF <int>, RA <int>, ER <int>, ERA <dbl>, CG <int>, SHO <int>,
#   SV <int>, IPouts <int>, HA <int>, HRA <int>, BBA <int>, SOA <int>, E <int>,
#   DP <int>, FP <dbl>, name <chr>, park <chr>, attendance <int>, BPF <int>,
#   PPF <int>, teamIDBR <chr>, teamIDlahman45 <chr>, teamIDretro <chr>


This is the same table from above, so it looks like I still remember how to write SQL! I’m going to use the dplyr workflow going forward because I’m more comfortable with it.

Hall of Fame Players
Here we’re taking a look at the HallOfFame table and doing some wrangling:

hof <- tbl(lahman, "HallOfFame") |>
         filter(inducted == "Y") |>
         select(playerID, yearID, category) |>
         collect()

hof

# A tibble: 340 × 3
   playerID  yearID category         
   <chr>      <int> <chr>            
 1 cobbty01    1936 Player           
 2 ruthba01    1936 Player           
 3 wagneho01   1936 Player           
 4 mathech01   1936 Player           
 5 johnswa01   1936 Player           
 6 lajoina01   1937 Player           
 7 speaktr01   1937 Player           
 8 youngcy01   1937 Player           
 9 bulkemo99   1937 Pioneer/Executive
10 johnsba99   1937 Pioneer/Executive
# ℹ 330 more rows


Next, we use the playerID key variable to left join the hof and people data sets so we have the first and last name for each player.

people <- tbl(lahman, "People") |>
            select(playerID, nameFirst, nameLast) |>
            collect()

hof <- left_join(hof, people) |>
       select(nameFirst, nameLast, playerID, yearID, category)

Joining with `by = join_by(playerID)`

Manager Statistics
We’re now focusing our attention on the Managers table:

mgrs <- tbl(lahman, "Managers") |>
        select(playerID, G, W, L) |>
        group_by(playerID) |>
        summarize(G_managed = sum(G, na.rm = TRUE),
                  Total_W = sum(W, na.rm = TRUE),
                  Total_L = sum(L, na.rm = TRUE)) |>
        collect() |>
        mutate(career_wl = Total_W/G_managed) |>
        arrange(desc(career_wl))

mgrs

# ℹ 739 more rows


Unsurprisingly, the highest career win/loss stats are held by managers who managed a few games to victory.
Lastly, we join the hof and mgrs tables by playerID to return all Hall of Fame members who managed games at some point in their career. I’m also reordering the columns so the order is more useful.

hof_mgrs <- inner_join(mgrs, hof) |>
            select(playerID, nameFirst, nameLast, yearID,
                   category, G_managed, Total_W, Total_L, career_wl)

Joining with `by = join_by(playerID)`

# be a good egg and close the db connection
dbDisconnect(lahman)
